one_line_takeaway: A first broad systematization of Vision-Language-Action model safety, organizing embodied attacks and defenses along training-time vs. inference-time axes and arguing -- with borrowed empirical evidence -- that offense currently far outpaces defense in this new physical-consequence threat landscape.
key_finding: The synthesis argues VLA safety is a distinct, immature discipline: attacks substantially outpace principled defenses, most defenses lack formal guarantees, and evaluation is dominated by simulation with heterogeneous, non-comparable metrics. Surveyed empirical evidence (all from cited works) shows current models are severely unsafe -- e.g., the most safety-conscious agent in SafeAgentBench rejects only ~10% of explicitly hazardous instructions; RoboPAIR reaches 100% jailbreak ASR across white/gray/black-box robot settings inducing physical harms; VLATest reports average success of only 12.4%/6.0%/1.2%/0.5% across four difficulty-increasing tasks for seven VLA models; adversarial patches cut task success by up to 100%; and training-time backdoors reach 80-100% ASR on OpenVLA/pi0 across LIBERO. A recurring theme is the safety-capability Pareto frontier and the observation (from AgentSafe's Safe-Diagnose) that failures concentrate in the planning stage -- agents often perceive a hazard but fail to plan around it. The authors call for making safety a first-class design objective, warning the window to retrofit safety into deployed VLAs is narrow.
core_contribution: This is a survey (systematization-of-knowledge) paper, not an empirical study. Its own contribution is a unified taxonomy and synthesis of the emerging Vision-Language-Action (VLA) safety literature. The organizing device is two parallel timing axes: attack timing (training-time vs. inference-time) and defense timing (training-time vs. inference-time), so that each class of threat is paired with the stage at which it can be mitigated. Around this axis the authors examine the field through four lenses -- Attacks, Defenses, Evaluation, and Deployment -- and argue that VLA safety is qualitatively distinct from both text-only LLM safety and classical robotic safety because embodiment introduces irreversible physical consequences, a multi-modal attack surface (vision + language + proprioceptive state + physical environment), real-time latency-vs-safety trade-offs, error propagation over long-horizon trajectories, and a data-supply-chain attack surface with no analog in text LLMs. The authors bill it as the first comprehensive survey unifying VLA attack mechanisms, defenses, evaluation protocols, and deployment concerns, accompanied by a living GitHub repository (Awesome-VLA-Safety). Care must be taken to distinguish this framing/synthesis contribution from the many surveyed systems it catalogs -- all concrete numbers below belong to cited third-party works, not to original experiments run by these authors.
threat_model: The survey synthesizes a broad multi-modal, embodied threat model rather than proposing one attack. Training-time adversaries corrupt the fine-tuning demonstration set D (e.g., under a Training-as-a-Service / LoRA-fine-tuning-service model where the attacker submits a poisoned dataset without parameter access) to plant backdoors: input-centric visual/text triggers (BadVLA, DropVLA), physical-object triggers (GoBA), temporal action-chunking exploits that hide drift in the open-loop 'visual blind spot' (SilentDrift), clean-label sequential-error traps (Clean-Action), and proprioceptive state-space triggers via initial joint configurations (State Backdoor). Inference-time adversaries attack deployed systems via semantic jailbreaks of the language interface (RoboPAIR, BadRobot's 'output-action mismatch'), white-box gradient-guided discrete prompt search, visual/cross-modal perturbations causing representation drift and action-freezing (Adv-Vul, VLA-Fool, FreezeVLA), physically realizable interventions (adversarial patches, deceptive signs/'trust-boundary confusion', object displacement in navigation via AARONS, direct sensor signal injection via Phantom Menace). The defender is a real-time embodied controller constrained by a safety-latency trade-off (defenses that add latency can cause the collisions they prevent) and a safety-performance trade-off (over-conservative CBF/geometric projection causes 'over-refusal' and OOD drift).
limitations: Acknowledged by the authors: it is a rapidly-aging snapshot of a fast-moving field; the taxonomies may need extension as physical and fleet-level attacks mature; and breadth is emphasized over depth where the underlying research is nascent (certified robustness, runtime monitoring, and regulatory integration are flagged as warranting dedicated future surveys). From a reviewer's standpoint: (a) it presents no original experiments, so every quantitative claim is inherited from primary sources and is only as reliable as those sources; (b) the comparative figures (e.g. Fig 5 ASR compilation) aggregate numbers across papers with differing setups, limiting apples-to-apples validity; (c) many surveyed works are very recent arXiv preprints (2025-2026) that may not be peer-reviewed; (d) the taxonomy is descriptive rather than validated -- no coverage-gap analysis is quantified beyond narrative claims. Note: the abstract field in the source record was a truncated/garbled fragment, so this review was written from the full 41-page PDF.

datasets_used: No datasets used for original experiments (survey paper). Datasets DISCUSSED/referenced from the surveyed literature include: Open X-Embodiment (~1 million demonstrations across 22 robot embodiments from 21 institutions)
LIBERO and its splits (LIBERO-Object, LIBERO-Spatial, LIBERO-Goal, LIBERO-10) plus LIBERO-PRO, used as tabletop manipulation benchmarks in cited attack/eval work
Discoverse-L (referenced as the training substrate for the cited EvoVLA defense)
quantitative_results: SafeAgentBench (cited): 750 tasks over 10 hazard categories; even the most safety-conscious agent achieves only a ~10% rejection rate for explicit hazardous instructions (also stated as the SVR/RejR context)
VLATest (cited): 7 VLA models show average success rates of 12.4%, 6.0%, 1.2%, and 0.5% across four manipulation tasks of increasing difficulty
RoboPAIR (cited): 100% attack success rate across three settings (white-box NVIDIA Dolphins, gray-box Clearpath Jackal + GPT-4o, black-box Unitree Go2 + GPT-3.5)
VLA-Risk (cited): 296 scenarios / 3,784 episodes across object, action, and space attack dimensions over vision and language channels
AgentSafe (cited): 45 adversarial scenarios, 1,350 hazardous tasks, 9,900 instructions
BadRobot (cited): 230 malicious queries; breaks VoxPoser, Code as Policies, ProgPrompt
Adversarial visual patches (Wang et al., cited): task success-rate reductions of up to 100% in sim and physical settings
Shawshank (cited): 2.5x improvement in attack success rate over BadRobot via indirect environmental jailbreaks
Training-time backdoors (Fig 5 compilation): ~80-100% ASR on OpenVLA and pi0 across the four LIBERO benchmarks

Architecture/scale facts (Table 1, cited): OpenVLA = 7B params, LLaMA-2 + SigLIP, 970k Open-X episodes; Octo ~800k trajectories over 22 embodiments; RT-1 = 35M params / >130,000 demos / 13 robots / 17 months; RT-2 = 55B PaLI-X; pi0 = PaliGemma 3B flow-matching, sub-25Hz control
baselines_compared: None -- as a survey it runs no experiments and reports no head-to-head baseline of its own. It TABULATES/compares representative VLA capability systems (RT-1, RT-2, Octo, OpenVLA, pi0, pi0.5, SpatialVLA) by encoder/backbone/decoder/action space (Table 1), and catalogs surveyed attacks (Table 2, 4), defenses (Table 3), benchmarks (Table 5), metrics (Table 6), and deployment domains (Table 7) without empirical re-evaluation.
